data.title: Hong Kong school hits back at filmmaker in row over Italian festival screening
data.description:
The supervisor of a prestigious secondary school in Hong Kong has hit back at the director and distributor of a controversial coming-of-age documentary, accusing them of “defaming” the integrity of the institution’s principal and placing her in an “unjust position”.
The claims, contained in a letter sent to students of Ying Wa Girls’ School on Monday, mark an escalation in the war of words over a screening of To My Nineteen-Year-Old Self that was planned at an Italian film festival that begins later this month.
The school’s supervisor, Reverend Eric So Shing-yit, wrote it was necessary to clarify the facts of the dispute, even though distributor Golden Scene had already announced the documentary would not be shown at the Far East Film Festival in Udine.
So said the school board had “absolute confidence” in the leadership and integrity of principal Tiffany Chuk Wai-kan, who was earlier accused by director Mabel Cheung Yuen-ting of “blatantly lying” about the arrangement for the screening.
He said Chuk had clearly conveyed to Cheung and a Golden Scene representative in a meeting on February 26 that “the school did not agree to, nor support, any form of screening until the school has obtained the consent of all major participants”.

So accused them of issuing statements that were “inconsistent with the facts”.
“[They] have defamed principal Chuk’s integrity and placed her in an unjust position,” he said. “For this, I solemnly clarify: principal Chuk did not lie, and all her professional conduct has been in accordance with the resolutions and expectations of the school board.”
During the meeting with the production team, they said they intended to enter the film in overseas festivals but failed to provide specific details, he added.
Chuk had stressed the need to know details of the screenings before a decision could be made, he said.
But as of last week, the school had yet to receive any relevant information about the festival from Golden Scene, So said, as he called on the production team to resolve differences with “an attitude of love and sincerity”.
The letter was issued four days after Cheung and six other members of the production team said they were “shocked and deeply saddened” by the school’s move to sever ties with them.
They claimed that during the meeting, the principal had asked the team to prepare a budget for the festival trip and raised no objections to the screening plan.
Cheung also said the principal had informed the students featured in the film of the plan in January and noted that all had agreed to the screening except for one.
As a result, all scenes featuring that student were removed from the version to be screened, she said.
The documentary, commissioned by the school, tracks the lives of six students over the course of a decade from 2011. It was released in 2023 and won Best Film at the Hong Kong Film Awards.
But it was then pulled from cinemas after one of the students featured said she had not consented to public screenings.
The student reported severe emotional instability after the film was shown at the school and said her objections were overlooked before its wider release.

data.title: China curbs live stream tipping with new rules to protect minors
data.description:
China’s internet regulator has introduced new rules for live stream tipping, banning children under eight from the practice and introducing a tiered system for older minors, as part of a broader effort to curb manipulative inducements and irrational spending behaviour.
The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) announced the live-streaming regulations on Monday after unveiling them on April 4, banning platforms from allowing children under eight to tip content creators. Under a new tiered system, platforms must obtain guardians’ consent before allowing users aged eight to 16 to tip, and must verify either guardians’ approval or proof of income for those above 16.
This marks an adjustment to a 2022 rule, which banned all minors from tipping live streamers.
The new regulations also require the tipping rules to be publicly disclosed and clarify the conditions for live stream accounts to enable monetisation features. Additionally, platforms must provide tipping limits and notification functions, regulate rankings and interactions, and establish a list of prohibited tipping and monetisation activities.


As the live-streaming industry has evolved in recent years, some streamers have used vulgar content or fabricated personas to induce, or even deceive, users into tipping, sparking a strong public backlash, the CAC said in a statement. “It is therefore necessary to introduce timely policies to systematically regulate live stream tipping.”
Since 2020, the CAC has conducted an annual internet clean-up dubbed “Qing Lang”, which translates to clear and bright. In last year’s crackdown, the CAC previously said it had sanctioned over 73,000 live-streaming rooms and 24,000 accounts.
The new regulations mandate that if an account has a record of violations, the live stream tipping privileges would only be restored three months after the expiry of the disciplinary period. If an account is muted, its tipping monetisation privileges would be suspended for up to two to three times the duration of the mute period.
The regulations make it clear that live-streaming platforms must prompt users to set tipping limits during their first transaction, and establish both daily and per-transaction caps.
They also stipulate that without users’ consent, online platforms are prohibited from disclosing users’ consumption statistics, such as tipping amounts and virtual gift purchases. Additionally, platforms cannot rely solely on tipping volume to rank or promote live streamers, nor can they use only spending data to rank users.
Online platforms are required to tighten oversight of tipping-related interactions and scrub illegal or harmful content. The guidelines also ban platforms from soliciting tips via cashback schemes or self-tipping and prohibit granting special privileges to high-spending users.

data.title: Firefighters prioritised tackling blaze over rescues, Tai Po probe hears
data.description:
Firefighters combating Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades prioritised bringing the blaze under control over evacuating residents due to the “unprecedented” scale of the disaster, a public inquiry has heard.
Four Fire Services Department officers on Monday shared how the operation unfolded during the initial stage of the 43-hour inferno that ravaged Wang Fuk Court in November, highlighting the difficulties in rescuing residents trapped in their homes due to the fire’s rapid spread across seven of the estate’s eight towers.
The then New Territories North deputy chief fire officer Raymond Wong King-man, who is now on pre-retirement leave, told a judge-led independent committee that the fire remained fierce for the first 10 hours after it started on the afternoon of November 26, posing immense challenges to firefighters.
The committee heard that by the time Wong signed off as commander overseeing the rescue effort on the morning of November 27, the department had handled 129 of 310 calls for help from people trapped inside the residential complex, but only 13 of those rescues were completed by 10pm the previous day.
“We always say it’s a two-track process. If you cannot put out the fire, how can you save those trapped inside?” Wong said.
The officer also highlighted the importance of fire prevention, noting that even the most advanced drones would have a limited effect on the blaze due to insufficient water pressure.

Two other commanders who briefly oversaw the November 26 operation said they had decided against helping with evacuations in the early stage of the blaze, as the fire was so fierce that resources could not be diverted to rescue operations.
Tai Po fire station commander Cheung Lok-hang said residents appeared to have learned of the blaze by their own means despite the fire alarm system’s failure.
“The difficulty that we faced was that the fire spread too quickly,” he said. “Our brothers had to suppress the fire by all means so that residents could evacuate. Doing anything else could undermine our effort to put out the fire.”
Cheung said the “unprecedented” fire, which killed 168 people and left nearly 5,000 homeless, had spread in all directions at a speed that exceeded the capabilities of normal operational strategies.
Committee member Chan Kin-por said “many residents” were anxious to know why the department did not use more water to combat the fire, but Cheung said the number of hosepipes used was already sufficient.
The station commander also dismissed the idea of dropping water bombs, saying helicopters might fan the flames and would have little impact in putting out the blaze indoors.
Senior station officer Hui Kin-on, who was among the first to arrive at the scene, said the department lacked clear guidelines on how to respond to a deactivated alarm system during a fire, relying instead on the commander’s deployment based on the circumstances.
Asked whether he could have warned oblivious residents using the fire engine’s loudhailer, Hui said it might not be as effective as imagined due to the loud noise at the scene, adding his team’s focus at the time was to contain the fire.
“The fire spread so quickly. I’d never seen something like this before,” he said.
Horace Lam Ho-chun, the department’s New Territories East deputy divisional commander, added that the sirens of fire engines and police vehicles could also have alerted residents of the danger.
The committee also heard on Monday that Sha Tin fire station officer Ho Wai-ho, who died in the blaze, might have entered the wrong building after helping a disabled resident escape and losing contact with his team members due to his unfamiliarity with the estate’s layout.
Hui, who tasked Ho with rescuing an elderly resident on the 27th floor at Wang Cheong House, said no commanding officers were present to keep tabs on firefighters entering the 31-storey buildings in the early stage of the blaze, as they were unable to get close due to strong flames and falling debris.
The witness said he was surprised to learn that Ho, who was thought to be the only firefighter inside the buildings at the time, had ascended to the 30th floor alone so quickly without realising he had entered the adjacent Wang Tai House by mistake.
The inquiry will resume at City Gallery in Central on Wednesday.

data.title: Hong Kong customs seizes 760 suspected endangered reptiles worth HK$580,000
data.description:
Hong Kong customs officers have arrested a male driver after intercepting a goods vehicle at the Shenzhen Bay checkpoint and finding 760 suspected endangered reptiles, with an estimated market value of about HK$580,000 (US$74,059), hidden inside.
The Customs and Excise Department on Monday said it found 106 turtles and 654 lizards hidden in his truck, believed to be endangered species protected under the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora (CITES).
Officers intercepted an outbound medium goods vehicle at the Shenzhen Bay control point last Wednesday and found irregularities in the truck’s undercarriage during an X-ray inspection.
“Twenty-six black bags were found concealed inside the chassis frame under the cargo compartment,” the department said in a statement.
“Upon examination, the bags were found to contain live turtles and live lizards of suspected scheduled endangered species.”
A 44-year-old male driver was arrested and later released on bail pending further investigations.
Anyone convicted of importing or exporting unmanifested cargo faces a maximum fine of HK$2 million and up to seven years in jail, under the Import and Export Ordinance.
Under the Protection of Endangered Species of Animals and Plants Ordinance, illegally importing, exporting or possessing endangered species carries a maximum penalty of HK$10 million and 10 years’ imprisonment.
Last month, the department seized nearly 1,000 turtles from the boot of an outgoing car at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge control point and arrested the driver and passenger.

data.title: Hong Kong home sales outlook firm even as La Mirabelle demand ebbs
data.description:
While Hong Kong developers have accelerated project launches following the Easter holiday, primary home sales are settling into a measured phase following an earlier surge in demand.
The shift is evident at La Mirabelle I in Tseung Kwan O, where Sino Land and its partners released a third batch of units on Sunday. Buyers snapped up 71 of the 261 units on offer, pointing to less urgent demand than in earlier rounds.
The latest batch comprises 33 one-bedroom units, 178 two-bedroom units and 50 two-bedroom units with a third room described as a study. With discounts of up to 15 per cent, prices range from HK$5.73 million (US$731,704) to HK$9.5 million, with per-square-foot prices between HK$15,335 and HK$19,613.
With demand holding up and mainland Chinese buyers remaining active, primary transaction volumes in the second quarter were expected to remain firm, though at a more stable pace as the market transitions out of its initial rebound phase, according to Centaline Property.
Midland Realty’s senior director Sammy Po described the pace at La Mirabelle on Sunday as “normal,” with full-day sales expected to reach around 70 units.
“Much of the initial demand had already been absorbed in earlier rounds, particularly from buyers seeking more attractive units,” Po said.
The more moderate take-up follows two earlier rounds of strong sales. In the second round, 152 of 168 units were sold in a single day on Tuesday, accounting for more than 90 per cent of the flats on offer and generating over HK$1.1 billion in proceeds.
Including the initial launch and units sold by tender, the project has sold 595 units in three weeks, raising more than HK$5.2 billion and making it one of the bestselling new projects in Kowloon so far this year.
Developers are still prioritising sales continuity, keeping pricing close to prevailing secondary market levels to ensure consistent absorption.

Recent launches illustrate this approach. In Kai Tak on Sunday, Wheelock Properties released a new sales arrangement for 32 units at Double Coast III, to be offered later this week, mainly one- and two-bedroom layouts. The project has sold a total of 491 units to date, raising nearly HK$3.82 billion.
Pavilia Farm III, jointly developed by New World Development and MTR Corporation in Tai Wai, also adjusted prices for 50 units at an average of HK$21,344 per square foot on Thursday, about 10 per cent below resale levels for earlier phases.
The pricing underscores a broader shift towards discipline, as developers focus on maintaining momentum rather than maximising margins.
The broader backdrop remains active this year. The first quarter, typically a slower time, recorded more than 6,300 primary transactions, the highest for the period since 2013. Home prices have rebounded by more than 10 per cent from their lows in March 2025, reinforcing a more stable market outlook.
The re-emergence of bulk buyers is providing an important layer of support, helping to sustain transaction volumes even as overall buying activity becomes more measured.
Hong Kong recorded 265 bulk-buying cases in the first quarter of 2026, the highest for the period since 2008, according to Centaline, which defines such deals as a single buyer acquiring multiple units in one development within a month.
These transactions involved 695 units worth HK$7.13 billion and accounted for more than 10 per cent of total primary market registrations in the quarter. The average investment per bulk buyer reached HK$26.91 million.
The number of such deals rose 1.3 times from a year earlier and increased sharply compared with 2023 levels, pointing to a recovery in investor participation. Sun Hung Kai Properties’ Sierra Sea project in Sai Sha led the trend, recording 166 bulk transactions involving 447 units and HK$2.75 billion in sales.
This combination of steady end-user demand and returning investors has allowed developers to continue pushing projects to market, even as the initial urgency fades.
At the same time, supply remains contained. As of the end of March, unsold first-hand units stood at about 17,589, still near a three-year low and down nearly 24 per cent from a peak in early 2025. Property agencies expect inventory levels to remain manageable if current sales trends continue.
Analysts said the impact on Hong Kong’s housing market from conflicts in the Middle East had been limited.

data.title: Hong Kong expands heat stress monitoring to better protect outdoor workers
data.description:
Hong Kong’s labour authorities have expanded heat stress monitoring to include data from 10 sites, enabling faster alerts to better safeguard outdoor workers.
Deputy Commissioner for Labour Vincent Fung Hao-yin said on Monday that the department would incorporate data from the Hong Kong Observatory’s newly added monitoring stations at eight sites across the city.
The move is intended to provide a more accurate picture of local heat stress levels and to issue warnings when outdoor workers should take breaks of at least 15 minutes.
The weather forecaster measures heat levels using the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature Index, which previously relied on data from detectors at King’s Park Meteorological Station in Tsim Sha Tsui and along the Beas River in Sheung Shui.
It has added detectors at its headquarters in Tsim Sha Tsui, as well as in Chek Lap Kok, Happy Valley, Kowloon Bay, Sha Tin, Wetland Park in Tin Shui Wai, Wong Chuk Hang and Kau Sai Chau, an island off Sai Kung.
With the enhancement taking effect next Monday, Fung said the department will use data from the new locations when assessing whether to issue a heat warning.
“Recently, [the Observatory] announced that they have additional stations receiving such data on heat stress, so that we are able to leverage on this new development, to make good use of this data, and to further enhance our heat stress warning system,” Fung said.
The commissioner said he believed the department would now be able to take a more “comprehensive look at the heat stress situation” across the city and provide more accurate information to the public once the new data was incorporated into the system.

According to the Observatory, the two existing monitoring stations would continue to operate, bringing the total number of locations to 10.
The three-tier warning system, introduced in May 2023, comprise amber, red and black alerts to warn outdoor workers or those working near heat sources to stop for between 15 and 45 minutes when temperatures exceed 30 degrees Celsius (86 Fahrenheit).
Fung explained that an alert would be issued when the heat stress level was triggered at four or more locations. Even if the detector at King’s Park Meteorological Station in Tsim Sha Tsui did not register the same level, an alert would still be issued to the public, he added.
Labour groups had previously warned that temperature readings varied widely between districts, and that detectors often missed conditions in remote areas.
Non-governmental organisation Oxfam previously called on authorities to add more detectors citywide to ensure more accurate tracking of heat stress.
In its latest report in January, the organisation deployed seven detectors in busy areas including Kwun Tong, Yau Ma Tei and Tsuen Wan.
The study found that over a 19-day testing period in August last year, detectors recorded an average of 14 days of amber warnings – 3½ times higher than the number of alerts issued by the department.
Fung said authorities reviewed the system each year and continued to refine it as more data became available from the Observatory.
“A review on the heat stress system has been continued over the years. We upgrade the system every year...and we hope to perfect the system as time goes by,” he said.

data.title: HKTVmall’s Ricky Wong says company aims to improve quality of life for elderly
data.description:
Hong Kong online shopping platform HKTVmall founder Ricky Wong Wai-kay has broken his silence over a science project involving controversial animal experiments, saying that the research he supported was meant to reduce elderly patients’ suffering from illness.
Wong, vice-chairman and group chief executive officer of Hong Kong Technology Venture Company, the platform’s parent company and a listed firm, wrote in a social media post on Saturday that its experiments were intended to improve the quality of life for the elderly.
“Initially, I had just one goal: to improve the quality of life for the elderly, so they wouldn’t have to suffer from illness or live each day feeling like they were just waiting to die,” Wong said.
He recapped how his career had evolved as his passion for technology moved from telecommunications to a concern for human well-being.
“From the beginning to the end, my interest is technology, from communication networks, television production and logistics automation in the past to medical technology today, it is all revolving around technology to improve human life,” he wrote.
“After turning 60, one by one, the elders in my family passed away. In the last few years of their lives, every little detail—daily routines, eating, using the bathroom, bathing, changing clothes—required someone’s help. Their world was confined to home and the hospital. Was that the life they wanted?”
The conglomerate revealed in its annual results in March that a four-year life science project involved 38 experiments testing the viability of detached animal heads and limbs.
The research team maintained a severed animal limb for about 46 hours and detached heads for about seven hours.
International animal rights group People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals Asia (Peta) last week staged protests outside the company’s headquarters and demanded a public boycott, condemning the decapitation tests as cruel and purely exploratory.
Responding to Wong’s post, Jason Baker, president of Peta’s Asia branch, said that recent transplant recipients of pig organs to humans died shortly after the procedures, demonstrating the failure of the approach.
“Real solutions to improve elderly care and increase organ availability already exist and don’t require animal suffering: investing in preventive medicine, expanding human-relevant research using organs-on-chips and other modern technologies, and reforming organ donation policies as other countries have successfully done,” he said.
Baker urged the public to stop supporting Wong’s online platform until the parent firm “permanently prohibits this cruel and misguided experiments”.
A spokeswoman for the conglomerate earlier said that the research it supported complied with local government regulations and focused on advancing human health through organ preservation without using pets.
In his post, Wong did not address any animal cruelty concerns directly but said his desire to improve people’s quality of life had led him to invest in medical solutions.
“Over the past four years, I have dedicated myself to medical research. I travelled to the West and established a medical research team of over 20 people overseas, including university professors, brain and neurology specialists, veterinarians, anaesthesiologists, and medical device engineers,” he said.

Wong began his post by saying that he had been “forced” into e-commerce, echoing statements in 2014 in which he said it was “an act of desperation” for his media company, Hong Kong Television Network, to launch an online shopping platform. The network turned to online and mobile streaming platforms after its application for a free-to-air licence was rejected by the government in 2013 despite massive public support.
“The results of our research can be applied to various medical fields in the future. University medical schools with a ranking of around 30 have already joined us in this research, hoping to use our technology for medical studies on organ and limb transplants,” Wong said.
The company’s annual report had indicated that its research team based its results on neuromuscular responses to electrical stimulation and electroencephalographic recordings, the corporate filing said.
However, its board admitted that the company could not accurately predict the project’s success rate or financial returns as yet.
Hong Kong Technology Venture Company recorded a net loss of HK$149.6 million (US$19.1 million) for the year 2025, while its gross merchandise value on order intake decreased by 1.9 per cent.
The company’s stock price sank by 11.5 per cent over the past month to HK$1.23 on Monday as markets closed, pulling its capitalisation below HK$1 billion.

data.title: Hong Kong court clears woman of forging qualifications in talent scheme application
data.description:
A mainland Chinese woman has been acquitted of forging academic qualifications in her application for Hong Kong’s talent scheme, with the judge saying she could have been defrauded by her agent during the process.
Xu Lina, 36, was cleared of any charges at Sha Tin Court on Monday after Magistrate Raymond Wong Kwok-fai said the defendant might have been negligent in failing to check her application form before submitting it to the Immigration Department, but her actions did not amount to knowingly and intentionally conspiring to defraud.
The magistrate said the prosecution was unable to prove the charge beyond reasonable doubt, leading to his verdict of not guilty.
Xu was charged with conspiracy to defraud after stating in her Top Talent Pass Scheme application that she was a graduate of the University of Technology Sydney, while she declared her education level as sixth form or non-degree tertiary education to immigration officers when applying for her Hong Kong identity card in April 2024.
Established in 2022, the scheme allows individuals with an annual income of HK$2.5 million (US$319,195) or above; degree graduates from a list of eligible universities with at least three years’ work experience in the previous five years; and degree holders from eligible universities with less than three years’ work experience who were not non-local students in the city to live and work in Hong Kong.
The court heard that Xu’s visa application was approved in early March 2024, and that she entered the city for the first time a month later to apply for her Hong Kong identity card. When completing the application, she again listed her education level as sixth form or non-degree tertiary education.
When Xu entered Hong Kong again a week later, she admitted to immigration officers under questioning that she had neither heard of nor attended the University of Technology Sydney and was subsequently arrested.

It was also revealed that when Xu read her application form after her arrest, she discovered that the address, phone number and email address attributed to her on the document were incorrect. The signature on the form also differed from her usual signature, the court was told.
Xu, who is married with three children and lives in Shenzhen, had learned about the talent scheme in 2023 from an acquaintance whose wife had successfully obtained a visa to bring their family to Hong Kong. The friend said Xu’s husband’s annual income would qualify them for the scheme and recommended the mainland agent he had used to the couple.
The defence said Xu paid a HK$300,000 deposit and sent passport photos and supporting documents to the agent, surnamed Sun, via WeChat. Xu did not read the application form or consult any official guidelines for the Top Talent Pass Scheme from the Immigration Department at any point.
Magistrate Wong said he found it “not unreasonable” that the defendant had not checked any information on the talent scheme after engaging an agent, adding that Xu’s declaration of her husband’s income as family income was acceptable as she had also helped in the business’s affairs.
“Since the husband had taken over his family business, the multiple transfers shown to the court showed that they were always earning money and had savings. In 2023, they had one payment of 5.41 million yuan to purchase a home in Shenzhen,” Wong noted.
“It seems that they could fulfil the HK$2.5 million annual income requirement.”
He added that since the contact details on Xu’s form were not her actual communication channels, all follow-up correspondence would have been directed to the middleman rather than Xu herself.
Wong concluded that it was possible for Xu to have believed her agent had submitted the application on her behalf using the family income as the qualifying criteria.

data.title: Mentally ill son who killed mother detained indefinitely at psychiatric hospital
data.description:
A mentally ill Hongkonger has been sentenced to an indefinite period at a psychiatric hospital for killing his 50-year-old mother and hiding her body under the bed while under the delusion that she was a “monster lizard”.
The High Court on Monday accepted recommendations from two psychiatrists and imposed a hospital order on Wong Chun-kit on one count of preventing the lawful burial of a body and one count of manslaughter based on diminished responsibility.
Mr Justice Wilson Chan Ka-shun said the case was a “tragic one” and the defendant’s mental state was “substantially impaired”, leaving him unable to distinguish right from wrong.
He cited the psychiatric reports, both of which indicated that the defendant lacked awareness of his own mental health condition and was unsuitable for outpatient treatment, leading to the recommendation for detention in a psychiatric facility indefinitely.
Chan ordered Wong to receive treatment at the Siu Lam Psychiatric Centre in Tuen Mun until he was deemed no longer a danger to society, rejecting defence barrister David Boyton’s plea for a seven-year jail term.
Wong, 28, had pleaded guilty to manslaughter last month on grounds of diminished responsibility, after prosecutors acknowledged he had suffered a relapse of schizoaffective disorder which substantially reduced his culpability in what was originally classified as murder.
Chan said the sentencing was the “most appropriate” both for public protection and Wong’s welfare.
Wong killed his mother, Sandy Lo Choi-kiu, 50, in a public rental flat in Upper Ngau Tau Kok Estate on November 24, 2022.
He initially told police he did not know what had happened but later admitted killing her, claiming she was a “monster lizard” and that voices had urged him to commit the act.
Wong, who was unemployed and relied on a monthly government allowance, had been under psychiatric care since 2015 for schizophrenia symptoms including auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions and irritability.
Before the tragedy, his mother had alerted psychiatric nurses that her son’s mental health was deteriorating and he had started speaking incoherently, even assaulting her.
After nurses visited their flat, Wong left home immediately and did not return until the following evening, after his mother filed a police report over his disappearance.
Later that night, Wong told two friends by phone that he was unhappy with his mother for “making a big fuss” by calling the nurses and keeping him from meeting a woman.
Relatives and hospital nurses lost contact with Lo on November 25. When an aunt and a security guard checked the flat two days later, the defendant said his mother was out and that he did not know where she was.
The hospital nurses alerted police on November 30 after hearing loud noises coming from the flat during a scheduled home visit. Police subsequently discovered Lo’s body under a bed.

data.title: Impact of Middle East war on Hong Kong’s aviation hub status ‘relative’: experts
data.description:
Passenger flight cuts triggered by surging fuel prices could temporarily affect Hong Kong’s status as a transit hub, but the impact will be relative and depend on how long the Middle East conflict lasts, aviation experts have said.
Steven Cheung King-lung, chairman of the Hong Kong Professional Airline Pilots Association, said on Monday that the city faced fierce competition in the region not only from Singapore, but also from the Greater Bay Area, as well as from Japan and South Korea.
“While Hong Kong is no more susceptible [to the impact of the war than its rivals], its competitive edge could be diluted,” Cheung told the South China Morning Post, noting that the city had been relatively slow in its post-pandemic recovery, compared with other places in Asia.
He said airlines would be inclined to consolidate flights with few passengers, but the reduction in local flights could also mean a longer layover for passengers, who may look for other options.
Hong Kong’s flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways said on Saturday that it would cut about 2 per cent of its flights between May 16 and June 30, mainly on regional routes, as well as a small number of services to Australia, South Asia and South Africa.
The carrier’s budget arm, HK Express, will also cancel about 6 per cent of flights from May 11 to June 30, with Cathay saying cutting capacity was a “last resort” it had to consider to “mitigate part of the increased costs”.
According to Cheung, airlines in Hong Kong were competing with those in Taipei and Tokyo for routes headed to the US and Canada, while the city faces rivals in Thailand, the Middle East and those on the mainland for European routes.
“Flight frequencies are definitely the core of an aviation hub’s competitive advantage. Once they are reduced, Hong Kong could be replaced by other cities,” he said, adding that the availability of shorter layover times elsewhere could cause business travellers to consider other options.
Hong Kong has been looking to expand its flight capacity, with the Transport and Logistics Bureau encouraging carriers around the world to fly to the city and the Airport Authority offering discounts ahead of the opening of the second terminal, according to Cheung.
He added that local carriers had also ramped up training, including for pilots, in preparation for the expansion of flights, but such efforts could be put on hold now as carriers cut flights to mitigate losses.
The Hong Kong airline sector would need to play catch-up when prices returned to normal, Cheung said, but he was still confident in Hong Kong’s long-term position as a top aviation hub, supported by strong demand and carriers’ long-term investments.

Law Cheung-kwok, a senior adviser at Chinese University’s aviation policy and research centre, told the SCMP that the status of any aviation hub was relative, as Hong Kong was not the only place with reduced flights. Many other popular hubs such as Singapore and those in the Middle East had similarly been affected by the war.
“The impact of the US-Israel war with Iran is global. The price for flight fuel has almost doubled. Carriers will calculate costs and reduce flights based on economic benefits,” he said.
“Hong Kong’s status as an aviation hub will not be affected by this war,” he said, though the impact would depend on the duration of the conflict.
Law said that it would take time for the Middle East to rebuild its transfer capacity after the war ended. This might benefit Hong Kong, as some travellers, such as those travelling between Australia and Europe, might prefer the city as a transit point.
Travellers from Hong Kong might also choose direct flights to their desired locations instead, which would bring business to carriers based in the city, he added.

data.title: Chinese EV stocks jump on surging exports, domestic demand hopes
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) stocks rallied against a falling broader market in Hong Kong on Monday, as strong export data and rising oil prices bolstered the appeal of battery-powered and hybrid cars while a coming wave of model launches sparked hopes of a domestic demand recovery.
Nio surged 6.6 per cent to HK$52 as of the noon trading break, while BYD climbed 5.6 per cent to HK$111, the highest since October 2. Chery Automobile rose 1.3 per cent to HK$32.72, while Xpeng advanced 0.5 per cent to HK$67.35 and Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology added 0.4 per cent to HK$55.05. Geely Automobile and Lantu Auto also traded higher.
Hong Kong’s benchmark Hang Seng Index slipped 1.2 per cent to 25,587.26 as of the break.
The rally came as fresh data pointed to strong overseas demand. China exported 2.23 million vehicles in the first quarter, up 56.7 per cent from a year earlier, according to data released by the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers on Friday.
Within the total, exports of new energy vehicles (NEVs) – a category that comprises pure-electric and hybrid models – more than doubled from a year earlier to 954,000 units, while exports of petrol-burning cars rose 29.9 per cent to 1.27 million units, the data showed.
The strong export performance soothed investor concerns about anaemic domestic demand and improved profit prospects for Chinese carmakers, according to analysts.
“The rising popularity of Chinese-made EVs in overseas markets hugely eased investors’ worries about the sector’s outlook this year as domestic sales slowed,” said Phate Zhang, founder of Shanghai-based data provider CnEVPost. “Chinese carmakers enjoy high profit margins abroad because they can command higher prices there.”
As the Auto China show in Beijing – one of the world’s biggest auto events – draws near, all major carmakers in China have announced plans to launch new models with the latest technologies.


The show runs from April 24 to May 3.
The new models, featuring more sophisticated self-driving systems and better batteries, were likely to whet buying appetite and drive up deliveries in coming months, analysts said.
Meanwhile, elevated global oil prices further strengthened the case for EV adoption overseas by expanding their life-cycle cost advantage over petrol-powered vehicles, analysts said, enhancing the global competitiveness of Chinese carmakers.
“Higher oil prices could trigger a non-linear increase in demand for new-energy vehicles once they pass consumers’ cost threshold,” said Dai Wenjie, an analyst at Shenwan Hongyuan Securities, in a note last week.
Companies with a higher share of overseas NEV sales were better positioned to benefit, with stronger earnings growth and valuation upside when oil prices stabilise at elevated levels, he added.

data.title: China’s energy bosses push for tighter rules as overcapacity bitesCalls for tighter approvals highlight how excess capacity is undermining growth despite global demand tailwinds.
data.description:
The chairmen of China’s battery and solar giants have called for more top-down regulations to rein in cutthroat competition, warning that unchecked overcapacity is squeezing profits and threatening the industry’s stability.
Despite tailwinds including the Middle East conflict that could increase demand for renewable products, overcapacity remains a major challenge for industry players.
Zhang Tianren, chairman of Tianneng Holding Group – ranked second behind Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (CATL) in a 2025 list of global top 500 new energy firms compiled by the state-backed China Institute of Energy Economics – urged authorities to tighten approvals for new projects.
“Policies should not only guide the industry but also provide a plan for it,” Zhang told the South China Morning Post. He said top-down planning was needed to avoid overlap and overcapacity in projects across mainland cities.
Zhang, whose company produces lead-acid and lithium batteries as well as industrial energy storage systems (ESS), said overcapacity in batteries was the legacy of decades of economic competition among local governments, which he described as the biggest issue. He warned that excess capacity now exceeded market demand by multiples, in some cases running into triple-digit percentages.

Although top policymakers mentioned “involution” for the first time in July 2024, addressing the need to tackle cutthroat competition, Zhang said there was still a long way to go to end domestic competition, even though the anti-involution campaign was taking effect.
Trina Solar chairman Gao Jifan, speaking as a delegate at the National People’s Congress in March, suggested banning new low-level production capacity without technological edge.
Longi Green Energy Technology chairman Zhong Baoshen advocated new standards and financial rules for the quality development of the solar industry.
In a written response to the SCMP, Zhong said there was an urgent need to establish mandatory sales standards for modules and incorporate technical quality into procurement evaluations, fostering a “quality-for-price” orientation.
Zhong, who leads the world’s second-largest solar manufacturer by shipments, also suggested China should introduce regulatory rules similar to the “three red lines” in the property sector to monitor financial indicators such as debt-to-asset ratios of solar firms and limit expansion of high-risk producers.
Batteries and solar power are among the major manufacturing sectors in China caught in overcapacity in recent years. Lithium batteries designed for energy storage logged an average capacity utilisation rate of only 50 per cent in 2023, while some solar manufacturers saw their utilisation rates slide below 40 per cent in 2024.
Amid the competition, manufacturers suffered profit squeezes. Tianneng Power International – the Hong Kong-listed entity controlling Tianneng Holding Group – recorded a net profit of 1.44 billion yuan (US$210 million) last year, 42 per cent lower than in 2021. Shanghai-listed Longi posted a net loss for the second consecutive year in 2025, estimated at up to 6.5 billion yuan.
The proposals came even as the energy bosses recognised growth potential from geopolitical tensions and global investment in artificial intelligence.
Tianneng’s Zhang said volatility in crude oil prices could be a new opportunity for China’s new energy industry to expand globally. Longi’s Zhong said demand from power-hungry AI would be inelastic and could extend into 2026 and beyond.
The capacity race that Zhang warned about appears to have resurfaced. Nineteen battery makers from CATL to BYD announced new projects in the first quarter to boost production capacity by over 900 gigawatt-hours, involving investments totalling more than 180 billion yuan, according to Shenzhen-based industry consultancy GGII.
Regulators, however, are stepping in. On April 9, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and three other agencies summoned power and ESS battery makers and vowed early intervention measures to curb irrational competition, including promoting production capacity warnings, regulation and guidance to local governments in attracting investments.

data.title: Licence of Hong Kong travel agency revoked after guide coerced visitors to shop
data.description:
A Hong Kong travel agency has had its licence revoked after one of its tour guides allegedly coerced mainland Chinese travellers into purchasing items while visiting the city.
The Travel Industry Authority on Monday said it had earlier launched an investigation into Star Link Travel Limited over four instances in which one of its tour guides was suspected of coercing inbound mainland travellers to shop.
The investigation found that between January and March, Star Link Travel failed to take “all reasonable steps” to ensure that participants in the tour group were not coerced into making purchases.
It also found that in March, the tour guide, named Kuk Wa, had made what it described as “inappropriate remarks” to visitors and threatened that those who did not make purchases would have to pay the difference in the tour price.
“After considering the investigation results, the Travel Industry Authority believes that the inaction of the travel agency and the tour guide’s remarks effectively coerced tourists into shopping, constituting a serious breach of licensing conditions,” it said.
“The travel agency and tour guide involved have severely damaged the reputation of the Hong Kong tourism industry.”
The authority said that it had revoked the licences of both the tour guide and Star Link Travel under Section 111 of the Travel Industry Ordinance.
It added that it would continue to follow up on the matter and did not rule out further disciplinary action.
“The Travel Industry Authority emphasises that all licensees – whether travel agents, tour guides, or tour escorts – must strictly abide by their licensing conditions when conducting business or performing their duties,” it said.
When considering licences for tour operators, the authority said it looked at whether the licensee had taken measures to ensure employees and service providers signed an agreement pledging not to coerce purchases out of visitors.
Licensees were also required to provide guidance or arrange training for employees and tour guides, as well as conduct regular reviews, it added.
Under the Travel Industry Ordinance, a licensed travel agent that breaches licensing conditions is liable for up to two years’ imprisonment and a fine of HK$100,000 (US$12,800).
Forced shopping tactics by local tour operators have long drawn the ire of both city and mainland authorities.
In 2013, the mainland banned such tours to Hong Kong and Macau following a string of high-profile incidents, and a year later regulators on both sides of the border collaborated to crack down on unscrupulous travel agents engaging in coercive behaviour.

data.title: No commander to oversee firefighters as flames blocked access, Tai Po probe hears
data.description:
Victor Dawes SC, the lead counsel for the committee, identified in his opening statement six “human factors” that contributed to the deadly blaze that broke out on November 26 last year when Wang Fuk Court was undergoing large-scale renovations.
These factors included the deactivation of the fire alarm system, empty fire services tanks, the use of combustible styrofoam boards to protect household windows during the renovation process and the use of non-fire-retardant scaffolding mesh.
Other failures involve the removal of fireproof windows from the buildings’ emergency staircases and the replacement of wooden boards that allowed workers to enter scaffolding from buildings, as well as the workers’ smoking habits.

data.title: How China’s EV battery king CATL powers up profits while EV makers struggle
data.description:
While China’s electric vehicle (EV) makers are struggling amid fierce competition, battery giant Contemporary Amperex Technology Ltd (CATL) is consolidating its dominance, building its profits while erecting technological barriers to increase its pricing power.
The world’s bestselling power battery maker for nine years in a row, CATL in March reported a net profit of 72.2 billion yuan (US$10.6 billion) for last year, up 42 per cent from a year earlier.
The figure is only 8 per cent lower than the combined profits of China’s top four listed carmakers – BYD, Chery, Geely Auto and SAIC – which reported net income of 78.6 billion yuan. CATL earned 2 per cent more profit than the 70.59 billion yuan made by the country’s 11 listed carmakers, including smaller and loss-making players.
Comparing CATL to BYD – China’s leading EV maker and also known for its low-cost batteries and strict cost controls – is instructive. In 2025, CATL earned 121 per cent more than BYD, widening from its 26 per cent advantage in 2024, as BYD ended a four-year profit run with a 19 per cent year-on-year slump amid EV price wars.
“The EV battery industry, with its high requirement for safety, technology and economies of scale, has formed a higher concentration than downstream car manufacturing, where different companies with different backgrounds have been drawn into much fiercer competition,” said Paul Gong, head of China auto research at UBS.
The top three players hold more than 70 per cent of the total market for EV batteries and about 35 per cent in the passenger vehicle segment.
CATL’s market share in the domestic EV battery market hit an 18-month high of 50 per cent in January, followed by BYD’s 17 per cent and Gotion’s 6 per cent, according to a report by HSBC Qianhai Securities.
With more than 54,000 patents awarded or under application globally as of 2025, CATL serves most of the world’s major carmakers, especially in the market for high-end passenger cars.
Concentration and scale were enhancing the pricing power of battery-industry leaders, said Yale Zhang, managing director at consultancy Automotive Foresight in Shanghai.

“The battery is the heart of an EV,” Zhang said. “As battery manufacturing involves strict quality requirements, it’s clear that leaders have a better reputation in this regard than smaller ones.”
CATL’s EV batteries contributed nearly 75 per cent of its total revenue last year, with a gross profit margin of 23.8 per cent, exceeding the highest margin among leading carmakers, 20.5 per cent.
The giant also outperformed its rivals in profitability, achieving an overall gross margin of 26.27 per cent last year.
CALB Group, the fourth-largest domestic EV battery manufacturer, reported a net sales margin of 4.7 per cent last year, though its net profit surged 148 per cent year on year to 834.6 million yuan. EVE Energy, the fifth biggest player, posted an overall margin of 16.17 per cent, despite a 1.44 per cent rise in net profit to 4.13 billion yuan.
In the near term, profit concentration is expected to continue amid the energy shock stemming from Middle East conflicts.
Gong of UBS said that recent high oil prices had demonstrated the strategic importance of EVs and battery energy storage systems, where Chinese companies led the world.
Analysts from HSBC Qianhai projected that CATL’s capacity ramp-up this year was likely to unlock more market-share opportunities, especially in energy storage systems and the European EV battery market.
In the long term, the edge that CATL built on the lithium-ion battery – which dominates the current energy storage and EV markets – could fade, as promising new technologies like solid-state batteries could achieve breakthroughs and change the game, said Automotive Foresight’s Zhang.
“If the industry were to shift to new batteries rapidly, all players would be back at the starting line,” he said.

data.title: China launches national plan to boost AI education
data.description:
China has launched a national action plan to embed AI across its education system, in a strategic push to future-proof its workforce amid intensifying global competition in advanced technologies.
The “AI+ Education” action plan, unveiled by the Ministry of Education and four other ministerial-level bodies, mandates the integration of artificial intelligence at every stage of learning – from primary schools to lifelong education.
The initiative builds on China’s long-term education plan to the year 2035, released in January last year, which identified AI as a crucial engine of educational reform to meet the needs of the digital economy and future industries.
Education officials framed the plan, which was released on Friday, as a direct response to fierce global competition, with major economies including the United States, the European Union and Singapore having launched programmes to boost investment in AI-related education and training. Beijing’s initiative aims to raise AI literacy nationwide as a crucial pillar of future economic competitiveness.
“By redefining the skills needed for the modern era, AI is forcing a systemic and fundamental overhaul of education,” they said.
Building nationwide AI infrastructure is a central part of the plan. Instead of relying on fragmented local projects, the central government said it would consolidate computing power platforms and data networks into a unified service – a centralised approach designed to ensure efficiency.
The plan sets out a tiered AI integration strategy across the education spectrum, progressing from early literacy to high-level industrial application.
At the basic level, schools will be required to offer courses that nurture curiosity and problem-solving skills. At the same time, universities will establish AI foundation courses to foster cross-disciplinary innovation, while vocational and lifelong learning programmes will emphasise the “intelligent transformation” of traditional roles and ensure broad public access to AI learning opportunities.
To support the transition, Beijing intends to overhaul teacher training by embedding AI literacy into certification and licensing requirements. By improving teachers’ technical proficiency, policymakers aim to equip front-line educators with the skills needed for AI-enhanced classrooms.
Financing for the plan will come from the central budget, with strategic funding reserved for major national projects supporting the development of new AI education platforms. Local governments and schools will also be guided to invest in the reforms.

data.title: Hong Kong bets on 36-fold surge in computing power to join top global AI hubs
data.description:
Hong Kong is betting on a 36-fold increase in supercomputing power to propel the city into the top tier of global artificial intelligence (AI) hubs, as it ramps up infrastructure to keep pace with rivals London and New York in the race for digital supremacy, the city leader has said.
In his keynote address at the opening ceremony of the 2026 World Internet Conference (WIC) Asia-Pacific Summit at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai on Monday, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu underscored the city’s infrastructure road map as a cornerstone of its bid to become a leading international innovation and technology (I&T) hub.
“We are formulating a comprehensive strategy that combines world-class infrastructure, cutting-edge research and development, and a culture of responsible innovation. Boosting our computing power, a key driver of AI development, is a top priority,” Lee said.
He noted that although the city currently operated at 5,000 petaflops of computing power following the launch of the Cyberport AI Supercomputing Centre in late 2024, the government was already setting its sights on a far greater horizon, centred on a new data facility cluster in Sandy Ridge within the Northern Metropolis megaproject.
“By 2032, this cluster alone will provide 180,000 petaflops,” he told an audience of more than 1,000 global delegates at the summit.
“That is 36 times Hong Kong’s current computing power.”
The infrastructure push aims to solidify the city’s position on the global leaderboard, where Hong Kong currently ranks third in the Global AI Competitiveness Index, behind New York and London.
The chief executive described the summit as a clear signal of Hong Kong’s rising stature as an international innovation and technology (I&T) powerhouse.
“No less important, it reinforces our deep integration into the nation’s development strategies. The country’s 15th five-year plan, approved last month, continues to back Hong Kong’s growth as an international innovation and technology (I&T) hub,” he said, adding that the support also extends to I&T development across the Greater Bay Area.

The development of the Greater Bay Area – a city cluster of 87 million people across Hong Kong, Macau and nine mainland cities – was anchored by projects such as the Hetao Shenzhen-Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone, Lee said.
Officially opened in December, the zone straddles the border to provide vital land for innovation and technology (I&T) development, foster synergy with the mainland, and serve as a global gateway.
The summit marks the second consecutive year Hong Kong has hosted the regional arm of China’s premier internet policy forum, held under the theme “Digital and Intelligent Empowerment for Innovative Development – Jointly Building a Community with a Shared Future in Cyberspace”.
The summit featured an expanded two-day agenda centred on the impact of AI on economic growth and security, with key highlights including the debut of a high-level ministerial meeting and the WIC Distinguished Contributors Gala, which welcomed global innovation and technology (I&T) leaders.
The second day of the summit will focus on specialised expertise, featuring six expanded sub-forums that examine the intersection of AI and societal growth.
These sessions will bridge technical innovation and practical application, covering topics such as AI agent innovation, AI security and governance and digital finance.
Beyond the technical sphere, the agenda extends to human-centric domains, including intelligent health and the digital preservation of classical texts, illustrating how technology can enhance both quality of life and cultural heritage.
These sub-forums will identify new economic growth drivers through global I&T collaboration and fostering international cooperation within the digital economy.
To translate these high-level discussions into action, the day’s core agenda is bolstered by a series of practical, affiliated activities, including strategic government-enterprise dialogues, capacity-building workshops hosted by the WIC Digital Academy, and a dedicated new technology exhibition zone designed to showcase real-world breakthroughs.
The summit coincides with the Business of Innovation and Technology Week, anchored by the InnoEX 2026 trade fair from Monday to Thursday at the Convention and Exhibition Centre.

data.title: Colleague Skill: AI tool said to harvest abilities goes viral in China
data.description:
An open-source artificial intelligence project aimed at harvesting human capabilities into reusable AI “skills” has gone viral in China, gaining traction as a meme among the country’s uneasy young workers as they face growing job insecurity amid rapid AI advances.
Supposedly, certain skills of luminaries such as Steve Jobs, spiritual figures like Gautama Buddha and ordinary office workers have been extracted into digital form and uploaded online, making these skills – such as Jobs’ product intuition – available for anyone to tap for free.
The project, called Colleague Skill, was a whim that was developed in under four hours, its developer Zhou Tianyi told the Paper, a local media outlet affiliated with state-backed Shanghai United Media Group.
Zhou, a 24-year-old engineer from the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, said its initial aim was to turn work communications, documents and experience into reusable skills to save human workers from repetitive work.
The tool was meant for when “your colleague quit, leaving behind a mountain of unmaintained docs”, Zhou wrote in a description of the project on Microsoft-backed Github, the world’s largest source-code hosting site. In such cases, the program would help “turn cold goodbyes into warm skills … and cyber-immortality”, he said. The tool was available in multiple languages including Spanish, German, Japanese, Russian and Portuguese.
The concept of a portable “skill” came from US AI start-up Anthropic, which uses the term to define a set of reusable capabilities that enable its chatbot Claude to handle specific workflows in a more structured and repeatable way.
Colleague Skill gained notice at a time when young workers in China are being squeezed amid a weak job market and growing concerns that rapid advances in AI threaten job security.
One viral social media post captured the zeitgeist with a picture of an empty chair beside a bare desk and a note reading, “My desk has been emptied and my skills have been uploaded”.
Zhou cautioned that the ability to extract human expertise was limited and that AI skills lagged behind the capabilities of real people, according to The Paper’s report.
They might be able to handle routine and repetitive work, such as answering frequently asked questions or summarising documents, but AI skills could not match a human’s judgment, creativity and adaptability, he said.
You Yunting, a Shanghai-based lawyer focusing on intellectual property, also warned about potential personal data and privacy issues, as some companies had reportedly asked their employees to turn in their skills before departing.
While it was legitimate for companies to claim ownership over an employee’s work-related intellectual output, there was a fine line between that and non-work chat records and personal emails stored on company computers, according to You.
Despite its initial concept of harvesting skills from colleagues, inventive Chinese users quickly expanded Colleague Skill to broader uses.

In addition to business leaders, popular derivative projects on Github soon offered skills that supposedly represented former lovers, for those who wanted something to remember their former flame.
The user or users who uploaded Steve Jobs’ digital persona claimed to have extracted the Apple founder’s product judgment and storytelling expertise, suggesting that downloaders could find real product opportunities thanks to Jobs’ understanding of human needs and his technology acumen.
A persona for Buddha, meanwhile, claimed to condense the classical Pali Canon, which is among the most sacred Buddhist scriptures, offering perspectives on life’s choices, obsessions, pains and relationships.
But it was just “AI mimicking Buddha’s thinking, and should not replace professional advice”, the creators said.

data.title: DeepSeek ramps up hiring ahead of V4 launch as chip strategy questions swirl
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence start-up DeepSeek is ramping up recruitment in Inner Mongolia, signalling a potential expansion of its data centre footprint ahead of the much-anticipated release of its V4 model.
The Hangzhou-based company is recruiting for two new roles – server maintenance engineers and delivery managers to oversee data centre launches – in Ulanqab, in Inner Mongolia, according to recent job postings.
The move marks the first time DeepSeek has publicly advertised on-site roles tied directly to its computing infrastructure. Previous recruitment efforts were largely focused on software engineers and AI researchers in hubs such as Hangzhou and Beijing.
The hiring push comes ahead of the expected debut of its next-generation flagship model, V4, more than a year after the release of its R1 model propelled the company into the spotlight.
The upcoming launch has fuelled industry speculation over how DeepSeek is sourcing and deploying computing power and AI chips for the new model.
A report by US news outlet The Information last week said V4 would run on Huawei’s latest Ascend 950PR processor, while a separate report by Reuters last month suggested the model had been trained on Nvidia’s Blackwell chips, the US firm’s most advanced processors to date.
DeepSeek did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The recruitment drive also highlights a broader trend among Chinese AI companies to lower costs by shifting compute-intensive operations to less-developed regions.
As part of efforts to address imbalances in computing supply and demand, China launched its “Eastern Data Western Computing” initiative in 2022, aimed at relocating data processing workloads from the resource-constrained east to energy-rich western regions.
Inner Mongolia – known for its abundant energy resources and relatively low electricity costs – has since emerged as a key hub for large-scale data centres, particularly for AI training workloads that require vast processing power.
DeepSeek has previously tapped the region through third-party providers. Hohhot, the regional capital, had hosted several computing clusters supplying the company since last year, according to local media reports.
These facilities are operated by domestic players including China Mobile, one of the country’s three major telecoms carriers.
